customer_name: Kay Rosaire and Clayton Rosaire Mowrey
site_name: Kay Rosaire and Clayton Rosaire Mowrey

citations.narrative:
During inspection, APHIS Officials noticed the perimeter fence behind the area where the lemurs and monkeys are held is
not three feet away from the primary enclosure. Moreover, where the perimeter fence reaches the Mandrill enclosure it
joins the enclosure with no perimeter fence behind. Having a perimeter fence in place protects the animals in the facility
by restricting animals and unauthorized persons from going through it or under it. Additionally, it serves as a secondary
containment system for the animals in the facility.
An outdoor housing facility must be enclosed by a fence that is of sufficient height to keep unwanted species out. Fences
less than 6 feet high must be approved by the Administrator. The fence must be constructed so that it protects nonhuman
primates by restricting unauthorized humans, and animals the size of dogs, skunks, and raccoons from going through it or
under it and having contact with the nonhuman primates. It must be of sufficient distance from the outside wall or fence of
the primary enclosure to prevent physical contact between animals inside the enclosure and outside the perimeter fence.
Such fences less than 3 feet in distance from the primary enclosure must be approved by the Administrator.
To be corrected by September 30, 2026.

During inspection, APHIS Officials saw multiple resting platforms with wooden boards that were worn and had multiple
sharp and jagged edges. The resting platforms were been used by the tigers and the sharp and jagged edges can cause
injury to the tigers. The facility must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals
involved. The indoor and outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to
protect the animals from injury and to contain the animals.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with Facility Representative.
Additional Inspectors:
Colette Kirk El-Amin, Supervisory Animal Care Specialist
Lemnique Wafer, Assistant Director n

narrative:
3.78(d) 
   
Outdoor housing facilities. 
During inspection, APHIS Officials noticed the perimeter fence behind the area where the lemurs and monkeys are held is 
not three feet away from the primary enclosure. Moreover, where the perimeter fence reaches the Mandrill enclosure it 
joins the enclosure with no perimeter fence behind. Having a perimeter fence in place protects the animals in the facility 
by restricting animals and unauthorized persons from going through it or under it. Additionally, it serves as a secondary 
containment system for the animals in the facility. 
An outdoor housing facility must be enclosed by a fence that is of sufficient height to keep unwanted species out. Fences 
less than 6 feet high must be approved by the Administrator. The fence must be constructed so that it protects nonhuman 
primates by restricting unauthorized humans, and animals the size of dogs, skunks, and raccoons from going through it or 
under it and having contact with the nonhuman primates. It must be of sufficient distance from the outside wall or fence of 
the primary enclosure to prevent physical contact between animals inside the enclosure and outside the perimeter fence. 
Such fences less than 3 feet in distance from the primary enclosure must be approved by the Administrator. 
 
To be corrected by September 30, 2026. 
3.125(a)  Repeat
   
Facilities, general. 
During inspection, APHIS Officials saw multiple resting platforms with wooden boards that were worn and had multiple 
sharp and jagged edges. The resting platforms were been used by the tigers and the sharp and jagged edges can cause 
injury to the tigers. The facility must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals 
involved. The indoor and outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to 
protect the animals from injury and to contain the animals. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with Facility Representative.

Additional Inspectors: 
Colette Kirk El-Amin, Supervisory Animal Care Specialist 
Lemnique Wafer, Assistant Director n
